item.title: Oh
item.content:encoded: I hadn't expected that SCP article would make me almost cry. But now ;_;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great work, you make me sad.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can never read this without thinking of "the power of the dog" by rudyard kipling. don't click unless you want to cry.

+ show block
– hide block

there is sorrow enough in the natural way
from men and women to fill our day;
and when we are certain of sorrow in store,
why do we always arrange for more?
brothers and sisters, i bid you beware
of giving your heart to a dog to tear.

buy a pup and your money will buy
love unflinching that cannot lie—
perfect passion and worship fed
by a kick in the ribs or a pat on the head.
nevertheless, it is hardly fair
to risk your heart for a dog to tear.

when the fourteen years which nature permits
are closing in asthma, or tumour, or fits,
and the vet's unspoken prescription runs
to lethal chambers or loaded guns,
then you will find— it's your own affair —
but … you've given your heart for a dog to tear.

when the body that lived at your single will,
with its whimper of welcome, is stilled (how still!);
when the spirit that answered your every mood
is gone — wherever it goes — for good,
you will discover how much you care,
and will give your heart for the dog to tear.

we've sorrow enough in the natural way,
when it comes to burying christian clay.
our loves are not given, but only lent,
at compound interest of cent per cent.
though it is not always the case, i believe,
that the longer we've kept 'em, the more do we grieve:
for, when debts are payable, right or wrong,
a short-time loan is as bad as a long—
so why in heaven (before we are there)
should we give our hearts to a dog to tear?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saddening story with an interesting concept behind this reality-bender. This reminds me of "When an unstoppable force meets an immovable object" saying from behind physics. However, in this case, it would probably be more similar to describing this as "when an unstoppable force meets an infinite force of gradual time."
+1

item.title: Re: The SCP Logo Pride colors?
item.content:encoded: Author's choice to include it in the coding.

item.title: The SCP Logo Pride colors?
item.content:encoded: Not trying to be rude or anything, but why is this page showing the pride logo?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
as someone stuck in her room right now while she's got a massive project due in two months due to some combination of adhd-anxiety-depression-cptsd, reading this

Dr. Hydock: […] what I want to ask is do you feel meaningfully different, SCP-2420? Certainly our conversations over the years have shown you take a turn. Do you believe it to be the medication, the anomalous effects, or was it merely being allowed to see your dog again?

SCP-2420: I don't know, Doc. Probably the latter? I don't know. I mean, do you want me to say whether or not you think my dog somehow cured my depression?

brought me to tears because something like that would be really nice right now. I hope John's okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is the first SCP that's made my cry…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remembered about this article because of the jamcon and I'm not afraid to admit it

Anyway, I just realized that I forgot to upvote this piece when I first saw it, so now I'm going to fix this.

My favorite thing about this article is that it tells an emotional story without making it seem forced or out of place. Sad themes are usually hard to cover, but this piece does it brilliantly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An SCP in less than ten words?

Huh? 2,923>10.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man temporarily turns other dogs into his previously dead dog.

An SCP in less than ten words? Seems more like this belongs in tales, especially since the writing is good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Every dog thinks he is the goodest boy, and they are all right

Quality feel material right here, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Try not to necropost. But thank you for necroposting because I wanted to say that this comment right here makes me sad we can't upvote comments. Solid gold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why would you do this to me man

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm Bi (Nearly Tri) Lingual if that helps lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one made me cry like a baby. I have never owned a dog before but reading this SCP made me want to kiss a photo of my pet bunny, who passed away for three years ago, and clutch it tightly to my chest. It was devasting having to let him go, so I can't even imagine the joy I would feel finding out that he crossed the line between life and death just to be together with me and my family again.

A strong +1

Also, John and Mattie you're doing amazing sweeties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I miss Chewey. He was such a good dog. Take my tears and drown this like in them.

Great SCP tear jerking without being cliche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good god, I'm sitting at work trying to hold back tears from reading this.
I wasn't expecting a train to Feelsville so it hit me hard. Here, take all my upvotes. And all the pats for that good girl.

item.title: Well done
item.content:encoded: I've…never had a pet. Animal stories don't usually get to me. But I have dealt with depression. This story hit me hard. Strong +1.

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Sure.

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show proposed edits
- Hide

To link to an article with a similar theme, I propose the following edit:

SCP-2420 was a human (formerly John █████) who created instances of SCP-2420-1, which took the appearance and memories of the former's pet dog, out of domesticated dogs (or Canis lupus familiaris).

to

SCP-2420 was a human (formerly John █████) who created instances of SCP-2420-1, which took the appearance and memories of the former's pet dog, out of domesticated dogs (or Canis lupus familiaris).1

Footnotes
1. Given the noted similarities in their manifestation of deceased pets, a possible link to SCP-3737 is being investigated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A solid scp that tells a beautiful story and made me cry thank you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ᵒʰ ᵐʸ ᵍᵒᵈ ˢᵒᵐᵉᵒᶰᵉ ᵍᶦᵛᵉ ᵐᵉ ᵃ ʰᵘᵍ

+1 instantly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mattie doesn't shed when she's magic. Other than that, I could change it if you want, but I'm genuinely not too concerned. I don't know from science and I messed up. *shrug*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Attempts at DNA testing were rendered void when it was discovered that nothing could cut SCP-2420-1's fur.

This really bugs me. There are other ways to get DNA apart from cutting hair; cheek swabs are an easier way than hair, and you can also get some DNA from stool. Plus, don't dogs shed?

I realize this is a really small detail in the scope of this, and this is very well-written, but this detail bugs me enough to give it a no-vote. I see this kind of thing all the time in articles, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my god. Oh my god.

From one dog lover to another, +1.

Hopefully +5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My God, that feels. I can't even express how much I admire you, that kind of writing is the hardest and you nailed it. Now let me cry.

+1, and I wish I could upvote it again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cried at the end of this. This is the officially my favorite SCP. He's not high and mighty or anything special. Just a simple man who doesn't want to forget that his dog crossed the line between life and death just to spend a full life with him. Let the poor man clean the toilets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna be heartless and ask what would happen if she met Shia LaBeouf.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of Sachi, my blind mini poodle. Not as well-liked as the other poodles, but hey she's looking out for me I think.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great, now I'm sitting in my school's library trying to hold back tears. This is going to be a fun day.
+1

On a more serious note, this is entirely accurate. There are times when I think about my dogs dying, and I often can't see myself surviving it emotionally. I mean, we got one of our dogs specifically to provide emotional support after I was hospitalized for depression; that's how much of an impact they can have on our lives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…. I need to go hug my dog now.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This really hit home for me.
I had a Golden Retriever named Murphy, who I got when I was young. I had him for 15 years, just died this summer and it was really sad for me.
They should make me an SCP for the amount of liquid coming out of my eyes right now.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for feels. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Outside of monitored contacted with SCP-2420-1, all domesticated dogs (or Canis lupus familiaris) are to be kept outside of SCP-2420's awareness.

I think you meant "monitored contact".

Now if you'll excuse me, I'm going to wipe the tears off my face and mash the +1 button.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As the owner of a dog on the verge of death [Peggy- she has cancer :( ] this one definitely hit me. It made me extremely, significantly pained, but that is where its brilliance lies.

Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love dogs. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read, I cried. I am on the bus. I'm blaming you if anyone asks if I'm OK.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally I have fulfilled my dream of making people cry in two languages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This got a translation on the Chinese site!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since people seem to like this, here's some spoilers. In case it wasn't completely obvious.

+ Spoipers
 - Hide

mattie isn't magic. scp-2420 isn't a latent reality bender. the emotional world can affect the physical. the magnitude of their love for each other, mixed w/ scp-2420's depression and mattie's tendency to bully other dogs. the anomaly is predicated on them having such an idiosyncratic relationship.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then your upvote is the most important one to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I struggled with severe cynophobia for most of my childhood.

And yet here I am, desperately attempting to maintain composure while I upvote.

Bravo. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I work with dogs. I've worked in pet hotels for about 4 years total, now. I've seen dogs of all temperaments and types. Each one of them has been like my own dog to me. And still, no matter how many of them I meet, none could ever take the place of my own dog. My border collie, who passed away about 5 years ago. When she was gone, it was the worst feeling. I felt like this SCP did. It still hurts to have lost her, even to this day.

Fantastic job. Brb, actually crying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Punched me in the heart. And made me hug my dogs. This may be one of the most believable interviews I've read on here, beautifully written.
A couple of minor tonal issues in the clinical section, but (perhaps uniquely) the emotional stuff overruled it, and was certainly not enough of a problem to prevent an enthusiastic upvote. Now I need to go buy doggie treats so they know how much I love them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When the fella can't see them anymore, they quit being his dog and start being normal dogs again. Dogs that can be dissected FOR SCIENCE!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When a dog is SCP-2420-1, it is Mattie. And it is indestructible. When it is not in the presence of SCP-2420, it is just a regular ol' dog. Those are the former instances. Those were the ones they could dissect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-2420-1 instances were completely invulnerable to any form of damage. Attempts at DNA testing were rendered void when it was discovered that nothing could cut SCP-2420-1's fur."

"Former instances of SCP-2420-1 that were dissected…"

What? This was a huge sticking point for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You are history's greatest monster. ;_;

There's a couple things I could knock, but really, those interviews are just so damn good. I will point this out:

People said I was lucky I didn't get, but I'd rather've gotten hit. Maybe I wouldn't've died.

There's one missed word ("hit") in the first sentence, and I'm not sure if "I" in the second is intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The difference being that those things seem impossible because they exploit the tricks our brain uses to make sense of visual information, whereas these pictures really are impossible. It's as difficult to conceptualize as a wholly new color would be, and it could have any sort of effects on the human brain/body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit. I'm not actually crying, but… Right in the feels. Neutralized SCPs almost always make me sad (1983 being a notable exception), but this…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone whose dog recently passed, I'll just say that I'm not crying. It's just raining. On my face. Stop asking.

(Also, you refer to Incident-SCP-2420-1 in the beginning of the second interview log, but it was actually SCP-2420-A. Fixed that for ya.)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I have trouble believing that a picture of a Saint Bernard fitting into a small space causes physical discomfort. People can look at impossible seeming things all the time without effecting vascular dilation. What if the photo was just photoshopped?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Upstream Color was too cerebral, and it gave me a headache" is metaphorical unless my head literally hurts if I watch the film.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just gonna change that to physical discomfort and mental distress, then. Also, yeah, it's not metaphorical. This, I know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are the headaches literal? In which case: "Subjects viewing photographs of larger SCP-2420-2 in situations that would be impossible due to the dog's size have reported mental distress and physical discomfort, including migraine headaches in 65% of cases."

If they are metaphorical then drop them completely, as it has no place in a clinical paper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Look right below this. I answered it for you, friend. Also what's a metaphorical headache?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am not Troy, but:

This resulted in headaches and confusion regarding certain photos and videos

Are these literal headaches or metaphorical? For whom did this result occur?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How can I turn your downvote into an upvote, Troy? What tone issues bother you so?
